title: How to read a painting
excerpt: Fifty famous paintings, read the way art historians read them: one detail at a time, with the camera doing the pointing. Then a wall of 881 more.
content_html:
sheets.works/data drops

Member? Sign in for all fifty

You read the little label, nod, and move on. Name, date, and not much else. Nobody tells you what you’re looking at. This one does. Fifty famous paintings, one bit at a time.

a sheets.works story

What the label leaves out

A museum label gives you a name, a date, and the words oil on canvas. It doesn’t mention that the couple’s shoes are off, or that two extra people are hiding in the mirror.

That information exists. It sits in catalogues and monographs written for people who already have the degree. So most of us stand in front of a famous painting for about a minute, nod, and move on.

An example

This is the back wall of the Arnolfini Portrait, 1434. The whole room repeats in the convex mirror, and two people stand in its doorway who appear nowhere else in the painting. They’re standing roughly where you are.

Above the mirror the painter wrote Johannes de eyck fuit hic. Jan van Eyck was here. Painters normally wrote made this. He signed it like a witness.

Nobody notices the two in the doorway on their own. Someone points them out, and then you see them. That is all this does, on fifty paintings.

The eight steps

The same eight work on a Bruegel and on a postcard. Do them in order. Look first, decide what it means later.

1what is thisthe name, the painter, the year. One sentence, out loud.

2first looklist what’s there before deciding what any of it means.

3who is herecount everyone. People, animals, reflections, backs of heads.

4what is happeningone sentence you could defend while pointing at the paint.

5the objectspick two things that look deliberate and ask what job they do.

6how it’s builtwhere does your eye land first, and where does it go next?

7when and wherewho could afford this, and who was meant to see it?

8the bit that sticksthe one thing you’ll still remember next week.

Nine paintings, read closely

Start anywhere. Each read takes about three minutes, the camera does the pointing, and the painting is yours to explore at the end. More are on the way.

The rest of them

881 open-access paintings in one room, searchable, uncropped. Open any of them and read it yourself. The eight steps ride along.

Or start at the first one and go in order.

content_html_zh:
sheets.works/data drops

成员？ 全部 50 名均已登录

你读了小标签，点点头，然后继续前进。 姓名、日期，仅此而已。 没有人告诉你你在看什么。 这个可以。 五十幅名画，一次一点。

Sheets.works 的故事

标签遗漏了什么

博物馆标签上有名字、日期和布面油画的字样。 它没有提到这对夫妇的鞋子脱了，也没有提到镜子里藏着另外两个人。

该信息存在。 它出现在为已经获得学位的人编写的目录和专着中。 所以我们大多数人在一幅名画前站大约一分钟，点头，然后继续前进。

一个例子

这是阿诺菲尼肖像的后墙，1434 年。 整个房间在凸面镜中重复出现，两个人站在门口，在画中其他地方都没有出现。 他们大致站在你所在的位置。

画家在镜子上方写下了“Johannes de eyck fuit hic”。 扬·凡·艾克在这里。 画家通常会写出这样的作品。 他像证人一样签了字。

没人注意到门口有两个人。 有人指出它们，然后你就看到它们。 这就是五十幅画作的全部作用。

八个步骤

同样的八幅作品创作于勃鲁盖尔的一幅作品和一张明信片上。 按顺序做。 先看一下，然后再决定它的含义。

1这是什么名字，画家，年份。 一句话，大声说出来。

2在决定其中的含义之前先查看一下其中的内容。

3在场的人数一下大家。 人、动物、倒影、后脑勺。

4发生了什么，你可以指着油漆区来捍卫这句话。

5 对象挑选两件看起来故意的事情并询问它们做什么工作。

6它是如何构建的？您的目光首先会落在哪里，然后又会转向哪里？

7何时何地谁能负担得起，谁应该看到它？

8 难忘的一件事——下周你仍然会记得的一件事。

九幅画，仔细阅读

从任何地方开始。 每次阅读大约需要三分钟，相机进行指向，最后您可以探索这幅画。 更多的内容正在路上。

其余的人

一间房间内有 881 幅开放获取的画作，可搜索，未裁剪。 打开其中任意一个并自行阅读。 八个台阶一路前行。

或者从第一个开始并按顺序进行。
